text: Here we present a case of a patient suffering from superior mesenteric artery syndrome managed with a robotic strong procedure.
Our patient was a twenty-six year old female who had a several year history of nausea, vomiting, postprandial abdominal pain, and flushing associated with an eighty pound weight loss.
She had previously been managed with a PEG-J tube and initially improved with gain of weight. However, her weight loss and symptoms recurred after the PEG-J was removed.
She had a CTA and upper GI series which were consistent with superior mesenteric artery syndrome.
Upper GI imaging reveals a focal narrowing of the third portion of the duodenum, with diminished transit of contrast visible. Dynamic imaging shows antiparastalsis occurring against the narrowing.

CTA demonstrated an aorto-SMA angle of approximately fourteen degrees, more acute than the expected angle of twenty-five to sixty degrees, and consistent with the diagnosis of superior mesenteric artery syndrome.

The surgical team places ports for the procedure, marking all robotic ports with an 'R'. The third robotic arm, labeled 'C', serves as the camera. Two assistant ports, labeled 'A-one' and 'A-two', are also utilized.
The surgeon begins the dissection by mobilizing the ligament of Treitz from the left side, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses Cotillier forceps in robotic arms one and two for dissection, while hook electrocautery is employed in robotic arm four. Bipolar cautery is occasionally utilized in robotic arm two.
The assistant uses atraumatic graspers to retract tissue, occasionally employing a ligature device for additional manipulation.
The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to dissect the peritoneum overlying the proximal jejunum at the ligament of Treitz, while the bowel is retracted inferiorly to aid in the dissection.
The surgeon preserves the inferior mesenteric vein, retracting it laterally and cephalad to prevent injury during dissection. The aorta is visible to the left of the proximal jejunum.
The surgeon continues the dissection, dividing the thin peritoneal attachments of the bowel beneath the superior mesenteric artery (SMA), using robotic instruments to facilitate the process.
The surgeon uses hook electrocautery to carefully dissect and remove adhesions from a previous laparoscopic cholecystectomy, revealing underlying tissue structures.
The surgeon identifies the lateral wall of the duodenum and uses a combination of electrocautery and blunt dissection to separate the duodenum from the transverse colon mesentery.

The surgeon medializes the duodenum, revealing the inferior vena cava. The dissection plane during the COCR maneuver is along the duodenum, with visible care taken to avoid injury to surrounding structures.

The surgeon mobilizes the third portion of the duodenum, proceeding cephalad along the anterior surface of the inferior vena cava. This maneuver facilitates medial retraction of the duodenum, allowing for the dissection of additional retroperitoneal attachments at the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon completes the dissection, creating a window posterior to the duodenum, revealing the aorta visible from the right side of the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon uses a Caudillere forcep and an assistant grasper to deliver the proximal jejunum through the retroperitoneum after completely taking down the ligament of trites.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to enlarge the retroperitoneal window, directing the dissection towards the patient's left side.

The surgeon mobilizes a vascularized omental flap to prevent the bowel from slipping into the acute angle between the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and aorta. The omentum is divided using a ligature and secured at the apex of the angle, modifying the original Strong procedure.

The patient was discharged from the hospital on postoperative day two, tolerating a regular diet with complete resolution of her preoperative symptoms. She is now home, no longer having postprandial nausea and vomiting.
Postoperative upper GI imaging reveals the duodenum and mobilized jejunum positioned within the right upper quadrant, with contrast visibly flowing freely through the small bowel.
We believe that in appropriately selected patients, the STRONG procedure is an appropriate operation for symptomatic relief and that it can be done robotically in the hands of a skilled surgeon.
